text: The laparoscopic procedure involves a splenic and vessel-preserving distal pancreatectomy. The surgical field reveals a four-centimeter cystic mass located in the pancreas of a thirty-one-year-old woman.
A liver retractor is placed through a right upper quadrant 5mm port to elevate the left lobe of the liver. The stomach is decompressed with the insertion of a nasogastric tube.

The surgeon begins dissection of the gastrocolic ligament approximately six centimeters from the pylorus using a harmonic scalpel. The dissection proceeds along the major curvature of the stomach in a cephalad direction, gradually exposing the pancreas.

The surgeon begins dissection of the pancreas proximal to the cystic mass using a harmonic scalpel at the superior border, ensuring hemostasis. A hook electrocautery is used alongside the harmonic scalpel for precise tissue dissection. The splenic artery is identified and carefully isolated using a Maryland dissector and hook electrocautery.

The surgeon uses a red vessel loop to encircle the splenic artery, obtaining proximal control and providing traction for subsequent vessel dissection. The loop is secured with a five millimeter endo clip.

The surgeon continues dissection along the inferior border of the pancreas using a harmonic scalpel, dividing the posterior connective tissue attachments with a hook electrocautery. The pancreas is elevated anteriorly, and a Maryland dissector is inserted behind it near the portal vein confluence. A medium-sized Penrose drain encircles and elevates the pancreas in preparation for transection.

The surgeon inserts a laparoscopic linear stapler with tissue reinforcement material, positioning it by elevating the pancreas using a Penrose drain. The pancreas is then transected, achieving good hemostasis at the cut edges.

The surgeon performs a careful dissection along the tortuous splenic artery and vein using a hook electrocautery and harmonic scalpel. The red vessel loop provides traction during the dissection of the splenic artery from the pancreas parenchyma. The dissection progresses toward the splenic hilum until the specimen is completely detached.
The video displays the intact splenic vessels alongside the dissected pancreatic specimen, highlighting the completion of the dissection process.

The operative time was approximately four hours, with an estimated blood loss of less than one hundred milliliters. The patient made an uneventful recovery and was discharged home on post-operative day three. Histopathology examination revealed a three point five centimeter mucinous cystic neoplasm with low-grade dysplasia. Proximal and distal margins are free of lesion.
